entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
@yrk_tkm : Yes, I started with a simple read-only one, then a complete one, then the most sophisticated one 🙂

Yes, I would say that vibing-steampunk is the best ADT-MCP Server so far.

I found the first one by asking an Agent in the Antigravity IDE. The second one was recommended by Perplexity AI, and I saw vibing-steampunk mentioned in a blog post by @Luisen in this very section.

As the next step I've been trying to link the adt-mcp with a Docu Server, so that Claude can look up documentation before performing a task. No luck so far.

entry.title: Re: 🇧🇷 Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Rio de Janeiro, Brazil)
entry.content:
Quero agradecer pela iniciativa e pela realização do evento “Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core” aqui no Rio de Janeiro. O conteúdo foi extremamente relevante, claro e inspirador, trazendo uma visão prática sobre o potencial da IA generativa no ecossistema SAP. Além disso, o networking proporcionado foi de altíssimo nível, reunindo profissionais engajados e dispostos a compartilhar conhecimento.

Parabéns pela organização e obrigado por promover um espaço tão rico para aprendizado e conexão. Que venham os próximos encontros.

entry.title: Request for Separate Exchange Rate Type for Purchases and Sales in SAP
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,

Currently, the system is using exchange rate type M for both purchase and sales transactions.

However, we require separate exchange rate types for purchases and sales to maintain different rates for these transactions.

Kindly suggest the best approach to configure this in SAP and advise whether we need to create new exchange rate types for this requirement.

Looking forward to your guidance.

entry.title: “Custom attributes” in ABAP platform workflows and related inboxes
entry.content:
SAP Business Workflow was first introduced in 1995 as part of SAP R/3 Release 3.0 (see: SAP R/3: Architecture, Function, Implementation" by Buck-Emden et.al..)

This longevity speaks to the robustness and deep integration of the tool in the ABAP platform – why also often the name ABAP platform workflow is used. A main goal was and is to distribute workitems (often also called “to dos” or “tasks” – don´t mix up this runtime artefact with the task-object from design time [e.g. TS30000016]) to the next worker to accomplish business outcomes in a coordinated way and as effortless as possible for human workers interacting with a digital system. Same is true for compliance approval processes implemented via flexible workflows in SAP S/4HANA CLOUD - we celebrate 10th Birthday this year.

Almost from the beginning, customizable task attributes and personalized worklist searching & sorting have been essential for enabling end-users to complete their work with freedom and flexibility—allowing them to work in the sequence their company or business process requires. This directly addresses all three psychological needs that Self-Determination Theory identifies as drivers of intrinsic motivation and self-actualization: autonomy, competence, and social relatedness. The concept of “custom attributes”: Company and business process-specific metadata that enables:

  • Task filtering and prioritization
  • Personalized worklist management
  • Context-aware task processing

This blog post deals mainly with the latest technology to define CDS Entity based custom attributes (which is not covered in the workflow overview training BIT602). For the sake of completeness, the older options are also mentioned or linked.

31 and also 10 years are tremendous for software components and tools. Congratulations to all contributed to that amazing story.

CDS Entity based custom attributes for My Inbox and SAP Task Center

CDS Entity-based custom attributes are the recommended approach for SAP S/4HANA Cloud and available sind ABAP Platform 2023. The CDS Entity must have identical key fields as the leading object associated with the workflow task or scenario. This method is supported for flexible workflow scenarios and their steps only.

Prerequisites Checklist

✓ Flexible workflow scenario with attached leading object

✓ CDS Entity key fields match leading object keys

✓ CDS Entity has @Workflow: { enabledFor: [ #CUSTOM_TASK_ATTRIBUTES ] } * key user possible [see section "Custom Attributes via key user extensibility" below]

✓ For amount/quantity fields: annotations include @Semantics

✓ Maximum 30 attributes per step (Task Center limitation)

Development

Note that it will only be possible to use the CDS based configuration for Tasks or Scenarios with an attached leading object.

At runtime, the system uses the CDS Entity name and field to populate custom attribute values. Configuration varies by deployment model:

  • SWF_USER_ATTR_DEF: Application development
  • SWF_USER_ATTR_CUST: Partner/OP customer development
  • SWF_USER_ATTR_CDS: Cloud customers (via SSCUI)

Note that the chosen CDS Entity has to have the annotation @Workflow: { enabledFor: [ #CUSTOM_TASK_ATTRIBUTES ] } if used in the transactions SWF_USER_ATTR_DEF, SWF_USER_ATTR_CUST or SWF_USER_ATTR_CDS. 

If the CDS entity field chosen is an amount or value field and carries one of the following annotations '@Semantics.amount.currencyCode' or '@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure' the corresponding currency or unit information will be additionally exposed to the frontend. The label of the corresponding column would in this case be taken from the '@EndUserText.label' given in the CDS Entity'.

Note that the annotation referencing the currency or unit of measure is evaluated during definition of custom attribute. This means that if any changes are done to those annotations in the CDS view after the custom attribute was maintained these changes will not be evaluated anymore. If such changes are done the custom attribute should be removed and recreated.

Custom Attributes Definitions created in SWF_USER_ATTR_DEF are not displayed in the transaction SWF_USER_ATTR_CDS but can be redefined using the button.

Definition and customization

SAP S/4HANA Cloud private edition provides three transactions for viewing, defining, and modifying CDS-based custom attributes:

Custom Attributes: Definition and Customization

Transaction Types

  1. SWF_USER_ATTR_DEF

 Purpose: Define custom attributes across all clients

  • Use Case: Typically used for solution delivery (e.g., by SAP Partners)
  • Transport Method: Workbench request
  1. SWF_USER_ATTR_CUST

Purpose: Define custom attributes for a specific client

  • Capabilities:
    • Define new custom attributes
    • Overwrite definitions from SWF_USER_ATTR_DEF
  • Transport Method: Customizing transport
  1. SWF_USER_ATTR_CDS

Purpose: Enable cloud customers to define or modify custom attributes

  • Availability:
    • SAP S/4HANA Cloud private edition (full transaction access)
    • SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition (via SSCUI - CDS view definition only) available from 2402 onwards over "Configure Your Solution"
  • Key Features:
    • CDS-based definition capability
    • "Convert into CDS based cust. Attribute" button to migrate from task container definitions
    • "Copy Entry from System Settings" button to replicate existing definitions

Important Considerations

Aspect

Details

CDS Definition Restrictions

Only allowed if no task container definition exists

Conversion Impact

Converting from Expression to CDS removes the original definition and cannot be reverted by cloud customers

Already Redefined Attributes

Do not appear in the selection screen when copying

Maximum Limit

30 custom attributes per workflow step (Task Center service limitation)

Recommendation

Define maximum 30 custom attributes per step

Granularity Options

Task Level

  • Expose the same custom attributes across all workflow steps using that task
  • Example: Task TS78500059

Workflow Step Level

  • Expose custom attributes for a specific workflow step (identified by Step ID)
  • Example: Task WS02000006, Step ID 1
  • Benefit: Useful when a task is reused differently (e.g., different approval perspectives)

Configuration Fields

Field

Description

Notes

Use Case

Groups or filters user attributes

Free text (max. 20 characters)

Name of Attribute

Unique identifier

Special identifiers starting with "custom" display in specific inbox locations

Description

Identifying text

Max. 40 characters; translatable via ABAP translation mechanism

Rank

Priority/importance indicator

Higher rank = higher importance; affects display order in Task Center

CDS Entity

Source data entity name

(CDS-based only) Must have same key as the task's leading object

CDS Entity Field

Specific field containing display data

(CDS-based only)

Expression

Container element reference

(Container-based only) Use format: &ELEMENT_NAME&; supports combined values

Type

ABAP data type

(Container-based only)

Field

External field type

(Container-based only)

Screenshots

 

 

Custom Attributes via key user extensibility 

WIth SAP S/4HANA Cloud , public edition 2505 and 2025 private edition key users can define custom attributes through the SSCUI in the Manage Your Solution app. If additional CDS Views are needed as a basis for their definition, you can use App Custom CDS Views to generate a CDS View by selecting the Custom CDS Scenario "Custom Attributes for Task." See detailed documentation. Note that CDS views generated this way will appear in the value help within the SSCUI. However, you must ensure that the key fields of the generated CDS view match the key fields of the workflow task's or workflow scenario's leading object. Additionally, the transport containing the generated CDS view should be released either before or together with the transport containing the SSCUI configuration.

Special Attributes in Custom Attributes Configuration

This part explains how to create complex custom attributes (with relations available with ABAP Platform Cloud 2402 /ABAP Platform 2025) beyond simple transaction-maintained ones:

Three Types of Special Attributes

Type

Annotation

Use Case

Value + Unit of Measure

@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure

Quantities with UOM (e.g., 10 KG)

Value + Currency

@Semantics.amount.currencyCode

Monetary amounts with currency (e.g., 100 USD)

Value + Description

@ObjectModel.text.element

Human-readable text pairs (e.g., Code + Description)

Please note, these related attributes are not available for My Inbox.

Key Requirements

CDS Entity Configuration

  • All referenced fields must be in the same CDS Entity, OR
  • Fields must be connected via an association with a single "on" condition

Text Arrangement (for Value + Description available from ABAP Platform Cloud 2405 / ABAP Platform 2025)

  • Use @ui.textArrangement: annotation to define display order
  • Default behavior: #TEXT_FIRST (description shown before value)

Language Considerations

Two Categories:

  1. Language Dependent
    • Example: itemCurrency, itemUOM
    • Evaluated per user's logon language
  2. Language Independent
    • Example: documentType, documentCategory
    • Same value across all languages

Language Management

  • Configuration Table: SWFCLANG (view: SWFCLANG_V)
  • User for Evaluation: SAP_WFRT logon language
  • Maintenance:
    • Private Cloud: SM30 transaction
    • Public Cloud: Configure Your Solution application
  • Storage: All texts stored with language keys per translation vector

 

Transport

Annotations and attribute configurations are transported together in the same transport request.

Sample CDS Entity and Custom Attributes

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'WF Demo: Leave Request Base (CDS)'

@ObjectModel.supportedCapabilities: [ #CDS_MODELING_ASSOCIATION_TARGET ]

@Workflow.enabledFor: [ #CUSTOM_TASK_ATTRIBUTES ]

define root view entity ZDK_WORKFLOWLEAVEREQUEST2
  as select from swxformabs

  association [0..1] to ZDK_DEMO_I_LR_TYPETEXT as _Text     on $projection.holidaytp1 = _Text.Type and _Text.Language = 'E'
  association [0..*] to I_Currency             as _Currency on $projection.Currency = _Currency.Currency

{
  key swxformabs.formnumber  as WorkflowLeaveRequest,

      swxformabs.approvby    as WFLeaveApprovedBy,
      swxformabs.createdby   as WFCreatedBy,

      swxformabs.addrnumber  as WFCreatorAddressNumber,
      swxformabs.pernr       as WFCreatorPersonalNumber,
      swxformabs.name        as WFCreatorNameText,
      swxformabs.department  as WFCreatorDepartment,
      swxformabs.costcenter  as WFCreatorCostcenter,

      swxformabs.firstday1   as WFLeavePartOneFirstDay,
      swxformabs.lastday1    as WFLeavePartOneLastDay,

      @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Text'
      @ObjectModel.text.element: [ '_Text.TypeText' ]
      swxformabs.holidaytp1,
      @EndUserText.label: 'LeaveRequest Type'
      _Text.TypeText         as LeaveType,
      swxformabs.reason      as WFLeaveReason,
      swxformabs.contact     as WFLeaveContact,
      swxformabs.procstate   as WFLeaveProcessState,

      case swxformabs.procstate
        when 'A' then 'Approved'
        when 'R' then 'Rejected'
        else 'New'
      end                    as WFLeaveProcessStateText,

      @EndUserText.label: 'LeaveRequest Currency'
      @ObjectModel.text.element: [ '_Currency._Text.CurrencyName' ]
      @UI.textArrangement: #TEXT_LAST
      case when swxformabs.holidaytp1 = 'R' then 'EUR'
           else 'USD'
      end                    as Currency,

      _Currency,
      _Text
}

 

Custom Attribute Types and Database Storage

Virtual Element-Based Custom Attributes

These attributes derive values from virtual CDS elements. The database record stores an exit-class name in the EXIT_CLASS field, which is used to calculate the attribute values.

Text Reference Custom Attributes

This configuration uses two database entries:

  • A primary custom attribute with REF_ATTRIBUTE_NAME pointing to a text reference attribute
  • A text reference custom attribute that provides the actual values
Currency-Based Custom Attributes

This structure requires three database entries arranged hierarchically:

  • Primary custom attribute (references a currency attribute)
  • Currency reference attribute (references a text attribute)
  • Text reference attribute (base values)
Unit of Measure Custom Attributes

Similar to currency attributes, this configuration also uses three entries:

Primary custom attribute (references a unit of measure attribute)

Unit of measure reference attribute (references a text attribute)

Text reference attribute (base values)

Implementation Considerations
Virtual CDS Elements

While virtual CDS elements are technically supported for custom attribute definitions, their use is not recommended for performance and maintenance reasons.

Runtime Behavior
Data Availability and Timing

Custom attribute values are calculated and stored when work items are created. However, not all container elements may be available at creation time, and CDS entity data retrieved at this point may differ from real-time values. Post-execution values and UI-updated attributes may not exist during creation, making them unsuitable for inbox display.

Dynamic Updates

Developers can update (available since ABAP Platform Cloud 2505 / ABAP Platform 2025) custom attributes post-creation using the CL_SWF_UTL_RUN_USR_ATTR_CHG utility class. After populating a local buffer with work items (via append_business_obj, append_workflow, or append_workitems), calling request_custom_attribute_chg asynchronously updates all non-final work items within that workflow

DATA(lo_util) = cl_swf_utl_run_usr_attr_chg=>get_instance( )
lo_util->append_workflow( '<workflow id>' ).
lo_util->request_custom_attribute_chg( ).

or business object.

DATA(lo_util) = cl_swf_utl_run_usr_attr_chg=>get_instance( ).

lo_util->append_business_obj( VALUE #( catid  = '<category_id>'
                                       typeid = '<type_id>'
                                       instid = '<business_object_instance_id>' ) ).
lo_util->request_custom_attribute_chg( ).

Performance recommendations

  • Include texts in CDS entities: Text fields should be incorporated directly into CDS entities used for customer attribute definitions (as shown in the example above). This should be documented.
  • Minimize CDS entities: Keep the number of CDS entities used for customer attribute definitions as small as possible—ideally just one. This should be clearly documented.
  • Push domain and reference table texts to database level: If texts from domain values or reference tables are needed, associate them at the database level by linking text associations to the CDS entity that collects these texts. Refer to `I_WorkflowStatusText` as a reference example.
  • Enable full buffering for text tables: Text tables should be configured for full buffering whenever possible.
  • Avoid CDS Virtual Elements: Virtual elements should not be used for customer attribute definitions. If a virtual element must be used, implement the `GET_CALCULATION_INFO` method to specify which CDS entity elements are required for data calculation.

My inbox

How custom attributes are displayed and consumed depends on the view of My Inbox that is being used.

Master-Detail View

The Master-Detail View is the standard view of My Inbox in ABAP Platform. See further details here: Master-Detail View.

When using some predefined names in the definition of custom attributes, some labels in the My Inbox UI can be replaced with the exposed data.

You can find further details in this documentation: Display Custom Attributes in the List View.

Screenshot.

Since ABAP Platform 2102 and ABAP Platform 2021, when performing a mass action (decision), custom attributes are exposed to the user in the multi-selection view. The table in the multi-selection view can be sorted by the custom attributes and also be personalized.

Screenshot

Expert View

The Expert View offers a tabular representation of the standard and the exposed custom attributes of the workflow tasks. The attributes are displayed as columns that can be personalized, sorted, grouped and filtered.

See further details here: Expert View.

SAP Task Center

For tasks to be available in SAP Task Center via the integration also, the defined custom attributes will be exposed to Task Center and be persisted together with the standard attributes in the cache of the Task Center Service on the SAP Business Technology Platform.

The cache of SAP Task Center task is then used by the UIs (e.g. the Web App and SAP Mobile Start) without having to additionally call the task providers. How the custom attributes are shown depends on the connected UI. 

Container based custom attributes

The second option defining custom attributes is to use a reference to particular elements of the workflow container.
Once the task is created they are stored as name-value-pair in the database. As opposed to the container, which is commonly stored as XML, custom attributes can be read via a simple database select.

Following elements can be used:

  • Plain elements
  • Table of plain elements
  • Object attributes (which are plain elements or a table of plain elements) 

Structure elements are not supported. Only simple fields of the structure can be used. The supported data types are:

C

Character string

N

Character string with digits only

D

Date (YYYYMMDD)

T

Time (HHMMSS)

I

Integer number (4-byte integer with sign)

b

1-byte integer, whole number <= 254

s

2-byte integer, only for length field before LCHR or LRAW

P

Packed number

F

Floating point number to accuracy of 8 bytes

g

Character string with variable length (ABAP type STRING)

 

How custom attributes can be defined depends on the UI in which they should be shown.

Please check these two blogs for more details:

1. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/embellishing-your-approvals-without-coding-my-inbox/ba-p/13323357

2. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-fiori-my-inbox-2-0-sp02-features/ba-p/13228953

Exit based custom attributes for My Inbox via Task Gateway Configuration

In general the definition of custom attributes via the configuration in SAP Business Workflow should be preferred to the configuration within Task Gateway since:

Activating a BAdI implementation for a task definition invalidates the definitions and customizations that were done for that task definition in SAP Business Workflow. By that preventing extensions by customers.

Custom attributes that were defined by means of this BAdI will not be exposed to Task Center.

While strongly discouraged custom attributes can still provided at task instance level by implementing the /IWWRK/BADI_TGW_CUSTOM_ATTR BAdI in the /IWWRK/ES_TGW_CUSTOM_ATTRIBUTE enhancement spot. You can find more information here: Custom Attributes. 

 

History: dynamic columns in SAP Business Workplace

For Business Workflow it is possible to create custom columns.

Up to 12 custom columns can be defined using the transaction SWL1 or the menu path  Tools → Business Workflow → Development → Definition Tools → Worklist Client → Dynamic Columns for Worklist.

the following fields can be edited:

  1. User Name: Relevant user name or ‘*’ to support all users
  2. Task : Relevant task type
  3. Expression: Value that should be displayed in the UI.  As source a container element should be specified via its name enclosed into the ‘&’ character. Please ensure that the container element name is taken from the relevant task type, for example '&NAME&'
  4. Header: Label that should be displayed in the UI.

 

Please check these blogs:

1. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sbwp-customization-tip-for-inbox-customization/ba-p/13304850

2. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/display-dynamic-columns-for-business-workplace-in-the-uwl/ba-p/1288610

Thanks to all contributors to this features & blog.

entry.title: Re: Sales Force To SAP Business Partner Intergration
entry.content:
Dear @Ashish13,

Yes this integration is possible in Public Cloud using the standard Business Partner integration approach. The main starting point on the SAP side is communication scenario SAP_COM_0008 and the standard interfaces include API_BUSINESS_PARTNER (OData) and Business Partner SOAP replication. SAP can generate the Business Partner number internally, while external Salesforce customer/address identifiers should be handled as external reference values rather than SAP internal keys

High-level guide where to start:

  1. First align which data must be created in SAP Business Partner general data, customer company code data, customer sales area data, partner functions, and address-related data
  2. Choose whether Salesforce will send data to SAP using API_BUSINESS_PARTNER (OData) or the SOAP-based Business Partner replication service under the standard BP integration scenario
  3. In Public Cloud, create the required communication setup for SAP_COM_0008 using Communication Management and the relevant communication arrangement
  4. Map Salesforce customer data to the SAP Business Partner/customer model, including general data, company code, sales area, and address-related structures
  5. Decide that SAP will create the internal BP number through internal grouping, while Salesforce identifiers are stored as external identifiers for traceability and updates
  6. Confirm whether bill-to and ship-to should be modeled as separate partner-role assignments, separate business partners or multiple addresses depending on the business design
  7. Start with create and update scenarios for one customer, then test company code extension, sales area extension, address updates and bill-to/ship-to behavior
  8. Validate that the customer created in Salesforce is correctly replicated into SAP with the expected BP number, roles, organizational data and address handling

More details below:

  • Business Partner Integration

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/f7399c7374d3475a96a56a0856b5f3ee.html

  • Business Partner (A2X)

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/85043858ea0f9244e10000000a4450e5.html

  • Data Replication Using SOAP

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/ba5eb8a54eef4822b3de4e2958f32554.html

  • Business Partner Number Grouping

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/15c19fb1f02544eebafaf5953a8e14ef.html

  • Multiple Address Handling in Customer Master Data

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/f3289df37b79410590db2eca6e5fe6bc.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Access CDS View data through SAP Integration Suite fo...
entry.content: Hi @qiangwan87, Thank you very much for your detailed explanation and suggestions — I really appreciate the time you took to help. Unfortunately, in my company we do not have API Management licensed in our SAP Integration Suite tenant. Even though the UI shows “Integrations and APIs”, when I try to create API artifacts I receive the following error: “API artifacts are not available on your tenant. Availability of these artifacts depends upon the SAP Integration Suite service plan that you use. For more information about different service plans, see SAP Note 2903776.” Because of this, using API Management is not currently an option, and my company will not extend the license. What I have managed to do so far is establish the connectivity between SAP Gateway and BTP through SAP Cloud Connector. I created the system mapping as a Non-SAP system (because using ABAP System did not work for me when connecting to BTP, although I am not sure why). After configuring the virtual host and resource /sap/opu/odata, the tunnel between Cloud Connector and BTP works correctly, and the destination test in BTP returns “Backend is reachable”. Since API Management is not available, I read in several places that it should also be possible to expose the OData service through a Cloud Integration iFlow acting as a proxy. However, I have not been able to successfully deploy the iFlow. The flow I attempted is: HTTPS Sender → Start → Request Reply → HTTP Receiver But the HTTP connection between Request Reply and Receiver shows a red error icon, which prevents the iFlow from deploying (I will attach a screenshot). Could you please advise: Whether it is actually possible to expose SAP Gateway OData through Cloud Integration alone (without API Management) in this scenario? If so, what should be the correct connections between Sender → Start, Start → Request Reply, and Request Reply → Receiver? Is there any specific configuration required for the HTTP receiver adapter when using Cloud Connector that might cause the validation error I am seeing? Thank you again for your help — it is greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: PM-Revisions not seen in OIOB
entry.content: Hi Anirudh, the reason is a selection issue. Where in ECC the revisions were selected separately table by table, in S4, they are selected in one inner join where one of the talbes is empty. SAP Note 3418241 is helpful here. Please check this SAP Note

entry.title: Re: Recurring Entry Approval Workflow in SAP S/4HANA - FBD1/FBD2/FBD9 Transactions
entry.content:
Thanks for sharing!

The code examples are very dated though. This looks like ChatGPT generated code, it always produces terrible quality ABAP code out of the box: lots of hard-coding, old FMs, subroutines, obsolete syntax. Might want to update your prompt and ask not to do that.

Please refer to Clean ABAP for code quality guidance: https://github.com/SAP/styleguides/blob/main/clean-abap/CleanABAP.md

There is also a good quick guide on Modern ABAP (this code is like 2 generations behind): https://www.brandeis.de/en/blog/cheat-sheet-modern-abap-en/

entry.title: What's New for SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Discovery in SAP LeanIX
entry.content:
If you've been following my previous posts, you know I joined SAP LeanIX for making your custom-built extensions deployed on SAP BTP transparent inside SAP LeanIX Application Portfolio Management. In June 2025, I wrote about managing SAP Build projects as part of your enterprise architecture. In November, I summarized how to create transparency across your SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry environments. Both avenues can be combined and share the same thread: If your organization builds on SAP BTP, your enterprise architects need visibility into what's running there.

Since the launch of providing insights into SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry runtime and environment, we've shipped two improvements that make the integration easier to set up and more insightful to use. Let me walk you through both.

1. No more certificate file juggling

If you've configured the Cloud Foundry integration before, you know the authentication steps. You had to create a platform user in SAP Cloud Identity Services, generate a certificate file in the user's profile page, set a password for it, download it, and then upload it into SAP LeanIX. It works and is a well documented path of authentication. But it also means keeping track of certificate files and passwords, and remembering to rotate them before they expire.

We've added an alternative: trusted certificate authentication.

Instead of uploading a certificate file and sharing its password, you can configure X.509 Client Certificates for User Authentication. SAP LeanIX generates a pattern that you copy into your IAS' trusted certificate configuration. That's it — no files to download, no passwords to store, no rotation to manage. As easy as that.

If your team is already using the SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery service for passwordless deployments to Cloud Foundry — as described in this SAP Community blog post — you're likely already working with trusted certificate authentication in IAS. The same pattern now applies to LeanIX' SAP discovery. That means one consistent, passwordless approach across your CI/CD pipeline and your enterprise architecture tooling: Your development teams deploy to Cloud Foundry runtime the same way your enterprise architecture tool can read its metadata. 

2. Keeping up with used CF buildpack versions

In my November blog post, I mentioned that Cloud Foundry discovery makes it easy to see which applications use which Cloud Foundry buildpack version. I wrote that "once the build pack ages, application owners can be asked to update." That was already useful for a point-in-time view. But what about keeping track of buildpack changes inside SAP LeanIX?

We've taken this further. The Cloud Foundry integration now automatically detects when buildpack versions change for your discovered applications and surfaces this information. This uses the same automatic version update mechanism we introduced for SAP Cloud ALM discovery. The logic adapts to whether an application has already been linked to a fact sheet or not:

  • Not yet linked: The discovery inbox simply shows the latest buildpack version. No action needed — when you eventually process the item, it already reflects the current state.
  • Already linked to a fact sheet: The system auto-updates the IT component with its version. If the update can't be resolved automatically, it flags the item as Review needed so you can resolve it manually.

 

Have you already used the SAP discovery for SAP Build or SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry? Leave a comment with your feedback! Last but not least, we work on the discovery of SAP BTP, Kyma runtime, too.

entry.title: Re: Connecting SAP Cloud ALM to On-premise systems
entry.content:
Hi, Adnan_Mohiuddin1,
There is not a limit to how many entries can exist for on-premise systems.  You will need to  execute /n/sdf/alm_setup on every SID:Client combination that you want to monitor in Cloud ALM Operations or use in the Change & Deployment Management landscape in Cloud ALM Implementation.

When you run through the configuration steps, mentioned in the section appropriate for the involved system at https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/setup-administration/connecting-systems-and-services, each system is registered with a unique LMS_ID which differentiates each SID:Client from the others.

If you have not found the solution to your problem, I would advise logged a support case so Technical Support can help resolve the issue.

Best regards,
Paula

entry.title: Re: Connect JDBC client - SquirrelSQL 5.0 or DBeaver - vi...
entry.content:
Hi Robert,

Your asesecurestore syntax is incorrect. It should be:

aseuserstore set mykey myhost:1600 user1 password

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ASE_SDK/c4016b5564ed4c98bbade36a3a35202d/165e3c08c8e44e7997829a64cc6cc810.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Examples for jConnect JDBC:

Properties props = new Properties();
props.put("SECURE_STORE_KEY", "DURIANDB");
Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:sybase:Tds:durian.sjc.sap.corp:1600",props);

DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:sybase:Tds:localhost:1600?SECURE_STORE_KEY=ASE1TESTUSER);

DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:sybase:Tds:?SECURE_STORE_KEY=ASE1TESTUSER);

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ASE_SDK/c4016b5564ed4c98bbade36a3a35202d/17c13174c0de42fb92adb65ecbe4dc91.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

The url will be given as normal for a JDBC connection to load and use jconn42.jar.
Then the connection property for the secure store is added as you would for isql (Open Client/CT-Library).
The secure store should work for all applications so the syntax is host:port.

Hope that helps,
Ryan

entry.title: Re: Request for Separate Exchange Rate Type for Purchases...
entry.content:
Dear @Surendarl,

In Public Cloud it is possible to create additional exchange rate types and maintain separate rates for them. However, SAP standard does not provide one single global setting to assign one exchange rate type to all purchase transactions and another exchange rate type to all sales transactions. The determination is process-dependent and differs between Finance, Sales, Purchasing and analytics

For Finance, SAP states that the exchange rate type for company code currency (currency type 10) is M and this cannot be changed in Public Cloud. If the business needs a different exchange rate type for parallel valuation or reporting the supported standard approach is to use an additional currency type in SSCUI 103379 Define Currency Settings for Ledgers and Company Codes, rather than replacing M for currency type 10

For Sales, SAP documents that the exchange rate type used in sales and service orders can be influenced by the Exchange Rate Type maintained for the sold-to party in Manage Business Partner Master Data. Therefore, if the requirement is to use a separate commercial exchange rate on the sales side, creating a dedicated exchange rate type for that process is a valid standard option

For Purchasing, SAP standard is more restrictive. SAP explicitly states that all Purchasing analytical apps use exchange rate type M and the exchange rate from the purchase order is not taken into account there, even if a fixed exchange rate is set. For supplier invoices, SAP documents that the system first checks whether a fixed exchange rate exists in the purchase order then whether a rate was entered manually on the invoice and otherwise determines the rate from the maintained exchange rates based on standard logic

Summary:

Keep exchange rate type M for standard FI/company code currency processing, create a new exchange rate type only where the consuming business process supports it, most realistically on the sales side and for purchasing, use the standard fixed/manual exchange rate handling at document level where a different negotiated rate is required, rather than expecting a separate global purchasing exchange rate type

More details below:


https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/f22f2aca75404b5f84becb9b246023b2.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/b4ee140eadb044cca34529f7c25f1e9b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/103f7a57015b8a1be10000000a44147b.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3512760

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3599406

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3681306

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3146709

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/01060a2e758f4e8a9d4a7cf07b0ce323.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Customer Experience Value Packages
entry.content:
This guide includes resources selected by the SAP Enterprise Support team to help you with SAP Service Cloud, SAP Sales Cloud & SAP Commerce Cloud. Some links require a one-time registration.  📌 Sign up | Walkthrough video   

 

Practical learning to help you improve customer outcomes

A sales leader wants better pipeline visibility.
An e-commerce manager sees customers abandon their carts.
A service manager struggles to meet response targets.

These situations are common. They also show where customer experience can break down.

The SAP Customer Experience Value Map offers Value Packages that help you address these challenges.

Each Value Package focuses on a real business goal. It explains the problem, shows the capabilities in SAP solutions that help solve it, and guides you through practical learning content.

You can start with a short blog to understand the scenario. Then you can explore the full learning content in our new Learning Platform.

 

Increase Sales Efficiency with SAP Sales Cloud

Imagine a sales representative preparing for a meeting. Customer data lives in different systems. Opportunities are hard to track. Forecasts feel uncertain.

This Value Package shows how SAP Sales Cloud helps sales teams stay organized and focused.

You’ll explore how to:

  • Manage accounts and contacts in one place

  • Track leads, opportunities, and pipelines

  • Improve sales planning and forecasting

  • Enable mobile sales for field teams

  • Use customer insights to guide conversations

These capabilities help sales teams spend less time on administration and more time with customers.

📖Blog overview: Increasing Sales Force Efficiency by Utilizing SAP Sales Cloud
🎓Access the Value Package in the Learning Platform

This learning journey explains how SAP Sales Cloud helps organizations improve productivity, strengthen collaboration, and make better sales decisions.

 

Improve Customer Satisfaction by Reducing Cart Abandonment

A customer adds products to their cart. The checkout process feels long. Shipping costs appear late. The customer leaves the site.

Cart abandonment often signals friction in the buying journey.

This Value Package explains how SAP Commerce Cloud helps teams improve the digital shopping experience.

You’ll learn how to:

  • Understand why customers abandon carts

  • Simplify the checkout experience

  • Improve product discovery and search results

  • Present clear product content and promotions

  • Use analytics to identify drop-off points

  • Offer real-time support when customers need help

Small improvements across the shopping journey can lead to higher conversion rates and better customer satisfaction.

📖Blog overview: Reducing Cart Abandonment with SAP Commerce Cloud
🎓Access the Value Package in the Learning Platform

The Value Package shows how SAP Commerce Cloud helps organizations create smoother buying experiences and build stronger customer relationships.

 

Improve SLA Compliance with SAP Service Cloud

A service team receives hundreds of cases each day. Some require urgent attention. Others wait too long in the queue. Customers notice when responses arrive late.

Service Level Agreements help teams set clear expectations for response and resolution times.

This Value Package explores how SAP Service Cloud helps organizations manage and improve service performance.

You’ll discover how to:

  • Define service levels and response targets

  • Track milestones for cases and tickets

  • Automate routing and escalation processes

  • Monitor service performance with dashboards

  • Identify patterns that lead to SLA breaches

With the right setup, service teams gain better visibility and can respond faster to customer requests.

📖Blog overview: Improving SLA Compliance with SAP Service Cloud
🎓Access the Value Package in the Learning Platform

The learning content explains how SAP Service Cloud supports consistent service delivery and helps teams meet customer expectations.

 

Continue Your Learning Journey

Each Value Package is designed to help you connect business goals with the capabilities available in SAP Customer Experience solutions. By exploring the blogs and the learning content in our new Learning Platform, you can better understand how these capabilities support improvements in sales efficiency, digital commerce, and customer service.

We encourage you to start with the blog that introduces the business challenge and the opportunity behind each topic. From there, you can continue with the Value Package learning content to explore the capabilities in more detail and see how they can support your organization’s CX priorities.

 💡  Have questions or feedback on this content?

Explore the SAP CX Value Map, which combines expert-led guidance with self-paced learning materials:

  • Blogs and Articles – Stay up to date with the latest enablement stories, tips, and insights from SAP experts.
  • Event Calendar – Join live sessions, webinars, and workshops designed to help you learn directly from specialists.
  • Learning Journeys – Build your skills step-by-step through structured, role-based learning.
  • Best Practices and Guides – Access ready-to-use materials that simplify complex CX topics.
  • Continuous Quality Check (CQC) Services – Keep your SAP CX systems running efficiently.

entry.title: Re: CDS view to link contract item to contract price records
entry.content:
Hello @Mario_Figueirin 

Thank you for the clarification.

I am not aware of any released CDS view that directly links purchase contract items to pricing condition records in the same way that I_PurgInfoRecdPrcieCndnAPI01 links pricing condition to purchasing info records.

Since the Public Cloud custom CDS views can only be built on top of released SAP VDM views and released associations, this means that if no released bridge object exists, then the relationship you are trying to model cannot be done directly in the Custom CDS Views app as a standard solution.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Authorization problem creating Purchasing Requests
entry.content:
Authorization issue: Users are able to create purchase requests in unauthorized purchasing groups. - Example: A user with the role BR_CENTRAL_PURCHASER_ can create purchase requests in purchasing group G40 using document type NB, despite restrictions.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Authorization problem creating Purchasing Requests
entry.content:
Hi,

If multiple catalogs sharing common authorization object to different roles with different restrictions, all these roles are assigned to same user, user will have a union of all these authorizations.

Hence, if Role A allows the authorization and Role B restricts and a user is assigned both Role A and B, then authorization status is allowed. Note that assigning multiple business roles to a business user increases the risk of overriding existing authorizations. 

For detailed information refer to How to Define Authorizations Based on Restrictions

In simple words, if one role restricts authorization and other role has unrestricted access- the authorization is considered as Unrestricted access.

Refer to 3411950 - Restrictions set on a particular field is not working as expected - SAP for Me

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: SAP CAP on HANA XSA: Scaling Industrial Data Applications Beyond the Cloud
entry.content:
In large-scale industrial data processing, handling high volumes of data is not an "edge case"—it is the standard. When thousands or millions of data points from sensors and machinery must be processed in real-time and synchronized via automated jobs, your architecture determines whether the system thrives or grinds to a halt. 

In this article, I will show you how we implemented a Mission-Critical Industrial Data Application using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) on SAP HANA 2.0 XSA (On-Premise). This combination is rarely discussed, as the CAP framework is primarily known for BTP-based development, but on-premise, it reveals immense power when paired correctly with HANA.

Why Customers Still Rely on On-Premise XSA

While the "Cloud First" strategy is dominant, many customers—especially in industries with critical infrastructure—continue to use SAP HANA XSA On-Premise. Key reasons include:

  • Data Sovereignty & Privacy: Some organizations handle highly sensitive industrial or national data that must remain behind the company firewall for security reasons.

  • Regulatory Compliance: Strict legal requirements in certain sectors prohibit the use of public cloud environments for specific types of operational data.

  • Minimal Latency: In industrial environments, being physically close to the data source (the machines and sensors) minimizes network latency, which is crucial for high-speed processing.

  • Legacy Integration: Direct proximity to existing On-Premise HANA 2.0 landscapes often makes a local XSA deployment more efficient than a hybrid cloud setup.

1. Visualizing the Architecture

To bring it all together, here is the architectural overview of the Industrial Data Platform:

2. The XSA Setup: Exporting CAP as a Module

Unlike in SAP BTP, the XSA environment requires an explicit configuration so the controller can correctly bind the service and manage port assignments. 

The Entry Point: srv/server.js

In an XSA infrastructure, the Node.js module must explicitly export its server handle. While CAP automates much of this in the cloud, on-premise XSA requires this manual step in the server.js:

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

// Optional: Bootstrap for custom middlewares (e.g., OData V2 Proxy)
cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    /* Custom logic, e.g., security headers or logging */
});

// CRITICAL for XSA: Export the server handle.
// This allows the XSA runtime to bind the service to the platform.
module.exports = cds.server;

In the package.json, we reference this via "start": "node srv/server.js".

The Deployment Blueprint: mta.yaml

The mta.yaml is the heart of the infrastructure. To handle parallel loads and optimize deployment, we use specific parameters:

_schema-version: "3.1.0"
ID: idp_platform
parameters:
  enable-parallel-deployments: true # Significantly speeds up the deployment process

modules:
  # --- Backend Module (CAP) ---
  - name: idp_platform-srv
    type: nodejs
    parameters:
      env:
        HDB_NODEJS_THREADPOOL_SIZE: "8" # Critical for parallel DB throughput
    build-parameters:
      commands:
        - npm ci # Guarantees exact versions (Deterministic Build)
        - npx cds build --production
    requires:
      - name: idp_platform-uaa
      - name: idp_platform-hdi

 

3. Evolution: From "Pure Node.js" to SAP CAP

Previously, such industrial solutions were often developed as pure Node.js/Express applications. However, moving to CAP offers massive advantages:

  • Efficiency: CAP provides the OData infrastructure and security (via @(requires: 'Viewer')) automatically.

  • Focus: We concentrate on the Domain Model (CDS), while the framework handles the "plumbing."

  • Hybrid Approach: Local development on SQLite, productive power on HANA.

 

4. The Database Layer: Native Performance with HDI

When millions of records are being ingested, JavaScript object mapping can become a bottleneck. Here, we "drop down" to the database level using specific artifacts in the db folder.

Configuration: .hdiconfig

For HANA to know how to handle our native files, we need an .hdiconfig. It assigns the correct plugins to the file extensions:

{
  "file_extensions": {
    "hdbprocedure": { "plugin_name": "sap.hana.di.procedure" },
    "cds": { "plugin_name": "sap.hana.di.cds" }
  }
}

Native Power: .hdbprocedure

By using a Stored Procedure, we leverage Table-Valued Parameters (TVPs) to pass data packets directly to the HANA core, bypassing framework overhead:

-- Anonymized Procedure for Mass Ingestion
PROCEDURE "proc_handle_mass_ingestion" (
  IN p_timestamps TABLE ( series_ID NVARCHAR(36), seq INTEGER, ts TIMESTAMP ),
  IN p_values TABLE ( series_ID NVARCHAR(36), seq INTEGER, val NVARCHAR(20) )
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT SQL SECURITY INVOKER AS
BEGIN
  -- High-efficiency batch insert using SYSUUID
  INSERT INTO "DATA_STORE_TIMESTAMPS" ("ID", "SERIES_ID", "SEQ", "TS")
  SELECT SYSUUID, series_ID, seq, ts FROM :p_timestamps;

  INSERT INTO "DATA_STORE_VALUES" ("ID", "SERIES_ID", "SEQ", "VAL")
  SELECT SYSUUID, series_ID, seq, val FROM :p_values;
END;

 

5. Automation & Real-Time: SAP Job Scheduler

To keep data current, we do not use simple internal app intervals. Instead, we use the SAP Job Scheduler. It triggers our import logic reliably from the outside at defined periodic intervals.

Once the job is completed and the mass data has been processed via the Stored Procedure, real-time updates are triggered in the Angular frontend. By using CAP events or targeted OData refresh triggers in the UI, we ensure that users always see the latest status of industrial assets without needing to reload the page.

6. Conclusion: The Best of Both Worlds

By combining SAP CAP's productivity with the raw performance of HANA Stored Procedures and the SAP Job Scheduler, the IDP platform manages massive data volumes with ease. We use CAP for 90% of the application and native HANA features where performance is the absolute priority.

How are you handling mass data imports in CAP? Ever experienced and/or applied SAP CAP on HANA XSA? I look forward to your comments!

Cheers

Rufat

entry.title: Re: SAP TM S4 Hana Public Cloud, How to distribute the co...
entry.content:
Dear @serchbrher,

In Public Cloud TM the Charges Profile / Distribution Profile does not offer distribution by net value / material price. SAP current official training content lists only these four standard distribution profiles for freight cost allocation: gross weight, net weight, gross volume and distance times weight so not seeing a Net Value option in your screenshot is expected standard behawior. SAP also describes the distribution logic as freight cost allocation from the freight order down to the relevant order/delivery items, controlled by the assigned distribution profile and again the documented options are only those four profiles

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-transportation-management/configuring-charge-management-and-explaining-the-charge-calculation-process

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-transportation-management/distributing-freight-costs-2

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Journal Entry Multiple Object Assignment with Statistical Projects
entry.content:
Hello dear SAP Gurus,

   As Cloud Public edition introduced, we know that internal orders are obsolete and no longer available. It means in account assignment block in journal entries with cost elements, a multiple account assignment with ( Internal Order + Cost Center ) or ( Internal order + WBS ) isn't managable, only ( Statistical WBS + Cost Center ) multi-assignment works for overhead costs and this leads to some questions in design of cost flow. 

   Since, our customer in need of tracking for example Vehicle Lease Costs in specific projects, lets assume proj.w/Rev. type WBS X, and wants to track in plate number detail, lets assume stat.proj type WBS Y created accordingly, a specific journal entry needs to be created as follows :

Journal Entry 123 - Actual Post -> WBS X, CElm A -> 100 EUR

Journal Entry 123 - Statistical Post -> WBS Y, CElm A -> 100 EUR

 Because multiple object assignment can not be done by same object type (In this example clearly WBS), we're expecting a solution. The requirement isn't critical and mandatory, yet meaningful and useful. Should we create a custom field to add another attribute or dimension to the journal entry and a CDS view for reporting ? Or should we seperate the statistical use of objects by the source of actual direct costs, for example :

Source of Actual Cost (Main Acc.Asg.) -> Cost Center -> use stat. WBS

Source of Actual Cost (Main Acc.Asg.) -> WBS -> use Cost Center statistical / by cascading assignment

Without detailing this logic for seperate business events and by keeping it simple, how can we achieve the solution with expected result ? 

   Thanks in advance for your guidance.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Finance SAP S/4HANA Cloud for enterprise portfolio and project management

entry.title: Re: SAP HCI - XML to Jason converter cannot handle nesting
entry.content: ML to JSON conversion issues in SAP HCI are quite common especially when nested structures are involved. Make sure your mapping is handling nested nodes properly — sometimes the standard XML to JSON converter in HCI flattens the structure incorrectly. Try using a custom script step in your integration flow to manually parse the nested format. Hope this resolves your issue!

entry.title: Re: SAP HCI - XML to Jason converter cannot handle nesting
entry.content: XML to JSON conversion issues in SAP HCI are quite common especially when nested structures are involved. Make sure your mapping is handling nested nodes properly — sometimes the standard XML to JSON converter in HCI flattens the structure incorrectly. Try using a custom script step in your integration flow to manually parse the nested format. Hope this resolves your issue.

entry.title: Custom Fields in SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM) consuming backend data
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am currently working on enhancements in SAP Service and Asset Manager (SSAM) using SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) and I need to add custom fields during the Notification creation process.

These fields already exist in the SAP PM backend, but I am not fully sure about the correct end-to-end architecture required for SSAM to consume and persist these fields.

My main questions are related to the correct flow between backend, OData service, metadata and SSAM configuration.

What I would like to understand is:

Backend requirement
If a new field is required for Notifications, does it need to be implemented in ABAP (for example via append structures or enhancements) and exposed through the OData service used by SSAM?

OData exposure
Once the field exists in the backend, is it mandatory to extend the OData service entity to include the new field so that it becomes available in the MDK metadata?

MDK metadata and application layer
After the field is available in the OData metadata, should it then be added in the MDK project by modifying:

Pages

FormCell controls

Create/Update actions

ConfigPanel usage
Some SSAM fields appear to be controlled via the ConfigPanel.

My question is:
Can the ConfigPanel be used to create and persist completely new custom fields, or is it only used to enable/disable fields that already exist in the metadata and application?

Entities in SSAM
If a field is not part of the current OData entity used by Notifications, is it necessary to:

extend the backend entity

regenerate metadata

and then bind the field in the MDK UI?

I would appreciate if someone could confirm the recommended approach for implementing custom fields in SSAM that interact with the backend.

Any documentation or best practices would also be very helpful.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: SAP DM and co11n parallel
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I would appreciate your clarification on whether SAP Digital Manufacturing (SAP DM) and CO11N in S/4HANA can be used in parallel for production confirmations.

In our current setup, certain critical machines provide real-time data through PLC and DCS integration. For these machines, we intend to capture production confirmations through SAP DM. However, some downstream machines, such as those used in later stages (e.g., packing and slitting), are less prone to downtime and do not currently provide data through PLC/DCS.

Given this scenario, I would like to understand if it is feasible to confirm quantities for specific work centers within a production order via SAP DM, while confirming the remaining operations of the same order manually through the CO11N transaction in S/4HANA.

Your guidance on whether this hybrid confirmation approach is technically supported and advisable would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards,

SAP Digital Manufacturing SAP Digital Manufacturing Insights

entry.title: Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Simone Azevedo
entry.content:
Este post faz parte da série “Somos SAP Community Curitiba”, criada para compartilhar as histórias das pessoas que ajudam a construir e fortalecer o ecossistema SAP na nossa cidade.


A ideia da série foi apresentada neste post de abertura:
➡️Introdução - Série da comunidade: Somos SAP Community Curitiba

A proposta é simples: responder algumas perguntas e contar um pouco da nossa jornada dentro da comunidade.


👩‍💻Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
Sou Simone Azevedo, consultora SAP e curadora da SAP Community Curitiba.
Meu papel na comunidade é ajudar a conectar pessoas, promover eventos e incentivar a troca de conhecimento entre profissionais do ecossistema SAP.
Acredito muito que comunidades são espaços poderosos para aprendizado, networking e crescimento profissional.


💙O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?
O que mais me motiva são as pessoas.
Cada evento, cada encontro e cada conversa mostram que quando profissionais se reúnem para compartilhar conhecimento, todos crescem juntos.
Ver alguém sair inspirado de um evento, criar novas conexões ou descobrir novos caminhos na carreira é algo que realmente faz valer a pena.


🚀Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?
A comunidade ampliou muito minha visão sobre o ecossistema SAP.
Além do crescimento profissional, ela me proporcionou algo ainda mais especial: amizades, parcerias e um verdadeiro senso de pertencimento.
A SAP Community Curitiba me ensinou que tecnologia conecta sistemas — mas comunidades conectam pessoas.


📸Um momento especial na comunidade


SAP Inside Track 2019 - 1ª Edição em Curitiba
Essa foto registra um momento muito especial da minha jornada na comunidade: a organização do primeiro SAP Inside Track em Curitiba.  Na foto, um time incrível de voluntários que ajudou a transformar esse evento em realidade. Muito obrigada! 💙
Meu agradecimento também ao @faustomotter (segunda foto) , pelo apoio e incentivo desde o início, e ao @Marssel700  , que mesmo atuando remotamente foi parte importante dessa construção.


🌐Conecte-se comigo
LinkedIn:
👉https://www.linkedin.com/in/simone-azevedo⁠


✨Convidados para continuar a série
Para continuar a série Somos SAP Community Curitiba, convido: @LuizSouza , @AdrianoCWB , @Marssel700 ,  @Raquelamar , @Fabiele 
para compartilharem suas histórias e experiências na comunidade.


Além das perguntas da série:
1️⃣Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
2️⃣O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?
3️⃣Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?


Deixo também uma pergunta adicional:
❓Que conselho você daria para alguém que está começando agora no ecossistema SAP e ainda não conhece a comunidade?


✨Frase final
A SAP Community Curitiba é feita por pessoas que acreditam no poder de compartilhar conhecimento, conectar profissionais e construir oportunidades juntos.
Cada história compartilhada fortalece ainda mais essa comunidade que tanto nos inspira.

#SomosSAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunity

entry.title: Error calling method "cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job" from RAP App
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I've been dealing with an issue when calling method "cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job" from the behavior implementation class of a custom RAP App.

The requirement is as follows:

  • The user will select a register from the report.
  • Based on the selected register, the program should print (in a physical printer) a PDF generated from an Adobe Forms. 

To do this, I've added an action in the Behavior Definition. This action (named 'Print') is called when the user selects a register and clicks a custom button.

In the implementation of action 'Print', we're trying to create a Job that will handle the calls to the necessary functions to generate and print the PDF.

METHODS print FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION label~print.

METHOD print.
lv_job_template_name = 'ZJT_MYJOB'.
ls_job_start_info-start_immediately = abap_true.
cl_apj_rt_api=>schedule_job(  EXPORTING    iv_job_template_name = lv_job_template_name
                                                     iv_job_text = 'SPOOL Registration Job'
                                                     is_start_info = ls_job_start_info ).
ENDMETHOD.

To create the Job, we've already created an Application Job Template, the corresponding Application Job Catalog Entry, and the implementing class.

However, when the app is executed, we are getting an error at the time method "schedule_job" is executed:

 I understand that the 'Insert' statement is causing the issue, but is there any way to work around this error?
